<details>
<summary>📝 Walkthrough</summary>

## Walkthrough

This pull request performs a comprehensive migration from Prisma ORM to Drizzle ORM across the entire database layer. The changes include updating package dependencies, removing Prisma schema files, creating a new Drizzle schema with table definitions and relationships, replacing all database query and mutation calls with Drizzle equivalents in multiple routers, updating the authentication adapter from Prisma to Drizzle, converting error handling from Prisma-specific error classes to PostgreSQL error codes, rewriting database seeding logic, and refactoring test utilities to mock the new Drizzle query structure. Documentation and configuration files are updated to reflect the new database tooling.

## Summary of Key Changes

**Configuration & Dependencies:**
- Added `drizzle.config.ts` with PostgreSQL dialect and schema configuration
- Replaced Prisma packages with Drizzle packages (`drizzle-orm`, `drizzle-kit`, `@types/pg`)
- Added `@paralleldrive/cuid2` for ID generation
- Updated `package.json` scripts to use `drizzle-kit` instead of Prisma commands

**Database Schema:**
- Removed `prisma/schema.prisma` entirely
- Created `src/server/db/schema.ts` defining all tables (`user`, `session`, `account`, `verification`, `genre`, `book`, `author`, `publisher`) with Drizzle ORM using PostgreSQL dialect
- Implemented table relationships and constraints including foreign keys, unique indexes, and composite constraints

**Database Access:**
- Converted `src/server/db/index.ts` from Prisma client to Drizzle pool-based initialization
- Updated all router files (`book.ts`, `genre.ts`, `user.ts`, `account.ts`) to use Drizzle query builders (`select`, `insert`, `update`, `delete`) with condition helpers (`eq`, `ilike`, `and`, `or`, `gt`, `gte`)
- Replaced cursor-based pagination logic to work with Drizzle's query API

**Authentication & Error Handling:**
- Switched `src/server/auth.tsx` from `prismaAdapter` to `drizzleAdapter`
- Updated `src/server/orpc.ts` to detect and handle PostgreSQL error codes instead of Prisma error classes
- Modified timing store keys from `prisma` to `db`

**Seeding:**
- Removed `prisma/seed/*` modules
- Created new `drizzle/seed/book.ts` and `drizzle/seed/user.ts` with Drizzle-based insertion logic
- Updated seed runner in `drizzle/seed/index.ts` to await async database client cleanup

**Testing Infrastructure:**
- Redesigned mock database in `src/server/routers/test-utils.ts` from dynamic Prisma proxy to static Drizzle-based mocking
- Added `chainResult<T>()` utility for creating awaitable mock chains
- Updated all router unit tests to mock Drizzle queries and mutations
- Replaced Prisma error code handling (`P2002`, `P2025`) with PostgreSQL codes (`23505`, `23503`)

**Documentation:**
- Updated `README.md` to reflect Drizzle as the database tooling
- Updated `.gitignore` to ignore Drizzle build artifacts and remove Prisma-related ignores

</details>

<!-- walkthrough_end -->

## Code Review

This pull request migrates the database layer from Prisma to Drizzle ORM, including schema definitions, seeding scripts, and router implementations. My review identified several issues: the keyset pagination logic in `getUserSessions` is flawed and requires a fix using the `lt` operator (which also needs to be imported), the seeding script for books suffers from an N+1 query pattern, the database error parsing is fragile, and several foreign key columns are missing explicit indexes which are necessary in Drizzle.

![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The keyset pagination logic for `getUserSessions` is incorrect for the `desc(createdAt), asc(id)` sort order. Using `lte(session.createdAt, ...)` in the `or` condition effectively swallows the tie-breaker logic because any row with an equal `createdAt` will satisfy the `lte` condition regardless of its `id`. This will result in duplicate items appearing across pages when multiple sessions share the same `createdAt` timestamp. The second part of the `or` condition should use `lt` instead of `lte` to correctly handle the tie-breaker.

```typescript
      const cursorFilter = cursorRow
        ? or(
            lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
            and(
              eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
              gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)
            )
          )
        : undefined;
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The corrected pagination logic in `getUserSessions` requires the `lt` operator, which is currently missing from the `drizzle-orm` imports.

```suggestion
import { and, asc, desc, eq, gt, gte, ilike, lt, lte, or, sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

This seeding logic performs an N+1 query pattern by calling `db.query.book.findFirst` inside a loop for every book in the dataset. This is inefficient for larger datasets. Since the `book` table has a unique constraint on `(title, author)`, it is recommended to use `.onConflictDoNothing()` on a bulk insert instead of checking for existence manually in each iteration.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Parsing database error messages using regex on the `detail` string is fragile as it depends on the PostgreSQL server's locale and version. While this works for standard English error messages, a more robust approach would be to handle constraint violations by checking the `constraint` property directly if the constraint names are known and stable, or using a specialized library for PostgreSQL error parsing.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Foreign key columns like `genreId` in the `book` table (and `userId` in `session`/`account`) should have explicit indexes for performance. Prisma automatically creates indexes for relation fields, but Drizzle requires them to be defined explicitly. Without these indexes, joins and filtered queries on these columns will result in full table scans as the database grows. Consider adding `index('book_genre_id_idx').on(table.genreId)` to the table definition.

🟠 **High** `routers/user.ts:285` The `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` condition in the `cursorFilter` is too broad. With `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`, items with the same `createdAt` but lower `id` than the cursor are incorrectly included in the next page, causing duplicates. For example, if the cursor is at `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 2)`, the `lte` matches `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 1)` which comes before the cursor. Use `lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` instead since the equal case is already handled by the `and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id))` clause.
🚀 Reply "fix it for me" or copy this AI Prompt for your agent: ```text In file src/server/routers/user.ts around line 285: The `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` condition in the `cursorFilter` is too broad. With `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`, items with the same `createdAt` but lower `id` than the cursor are incorrectly included in the next page, causing duplicates. For example, if the cursor is at `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 2)`, the `lte` matches `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 1)` which comes before the cursor. Use `lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` instead since the equal case is already handled by the `and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id))` clause. Evidence trail: src/server/routers/user.ts lines 270-310 at REVIEWED_COMMIT: cursor filter uses `or(and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)), lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt))` with `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`. The `lte` in the second OR branch matches rows with equal createdAt regardless of id, bypassing the tie-break condition in the first branch. ```

**Actionable comments posted: 2** > [!CAUTION] > Some comments are outside the diff and can’t be posted inline due to platform limitations. > > > >
> ⚠️ Outside diff range comments (2)
> >
> src/server/routers/book.ts (2)
> > `134-154`: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_ > > **`create` returns incomplete data—missing `genre` relation.** > > The handler's `.output(zBook())` expects a book with its related genre, but `insert(...).returning()` only returns the inserted book row without relations. This will cause output validation to fail or return incomplete data. > > Fetch the full record with genre after insert, or adjust the output schema. > > >
> 🛠️ Proposed fix > > ```diff > .handler(async ({ context, input }) => { > context.logger.info('Create book'); > const [created] = await context.db > .insert(book) > .values({ > title: input.title, > author: input.author, > genreId: input.genreId, > publisher: input.publisher, > coverId: input.coverId, > }) > .returning(); > > if (!created) { > throw new ORPCError('INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR'); > } > > - return created; > + const result = await context.db.query.book.findFirst({ > + where: eq(book.id, created.id), > + with: { genre: true }, > + }); > + > + if (!result) { > + throw new ORPCError('INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR'); > + } > + > + return result; > }), > ``` >
> >
> 🤖 Prompt for AI Agents > > ``` > Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. > > In `@src/server/routers/book.ts` around lines 134 - 154, The handler currently > uses context.db.insert(book).values(...).returning() which yields only the > inserted book row (no relations), but the route's output schema zBook() expects > the related genre; update the handler in the create operation so that after > insert you capture the inserted row's id (or primary key) from the returning() > result and then perform a follow-up read that selects the full book including > the genre relation (e.g., query the book by id and include genre) and return > that full record, or alternatively change .output(zBook()) to a schema that does > not require the genre relation—modify the code around the handler and the > insert/returning usage accordingly (look for the handler block that calls > context.db.insert(book).values(...).returning()). > ``` > >
> > --- > > `166-187`: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_ > > **`updateById` also missing `genre` relation in return.** > > Same issue as `create`—the `.output(zBook())` expects the genre relation, but `update(...).returning()` only returns the book columns. > > >
> 🛠️ Proposed fix > > ```diff > if (!updated) { > throw new ORPCError('NOT_FOUND'); > } > > - return updated; > + const result = await context.db.query.book.findFirst({ > + where: eq(book.id, updated.id), > + with: { genre: true }, > + }); > + > + if (!result) { > + throw new ORPCError('INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR'); > + } > + > + return result; > }), > ``` >
> >
> 🤖 Prompt for AI Agents > > ``` > Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. > > In `@src/server/routers/book.ts` around lines 166 - 187, The update handler > currently returns only the book columns from > context.db.update(book)...returning(), but zBook() expects the related genre; > modify the updateById flow in the handler so the returned payload includes the > genre relation — e.g. replace the single .returning() call with a query that > returns the book plus its genre (either by using a returning that selects the > genre fields or performing a follow-up select/join for genre), or use your ORM's > returningAll()/selectWithRelations helper to include genre; update the code > around the .handler, .output(zBook()), and the > context.db.update(book).set(...).where(eq(book.id, input.id)).returning() usage > so the response matches zBook() including genre. > ``` > >
> >
> >
🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents ``` Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. Inline comments: In `@drizzle/seed/user.ts`: - Around line 20-25: In the seed loop inserting into the users table using db.insert(user).values(...), Faker-generated emails can collide causing a unique constraint 23505 and abort the seed; update the insert to handle conflicts by calling the query builder's conflict resolution (e.g., chain .onConflictDoNothing() or equivalent on the db.insert(user) call) or ensure emails are deterministic/unique before inserting so duplicate email errors are skipped without throwing. In `@src/server/routers/user.ts`: - Around line 284-295: The cursorFilter in getUserSessions incorrectly uses lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt) which causes the tie-breaker and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)) to be redundant; change the second branch to lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt) so only strictly older createdAt rows are matched and the gte(session.id, ...) tie-breaker is applied when timestamps equal, and add lt to the imported operators (alongside eq, gte, lte, or, and) so the code compiles. --- Outside diff comments: In `@src/server/routers/book.ts`: - Around line 134-154: The handler currently uses context.db.insert(book).values(...).returning() which yields only the inserted book row (no relations), but the route's output schema zBook() expects the related genre; update the handler in the create operation so that after insert you capture the inserted row's id (or primary key) from the returning() result and then perform a follow-up read that selects the full book including the genre relation (e.g., query the book by id and include genre) and return that full record, or alternatively change .output(zBook()) to a schema that does not require the genre relation—modify the code around the handler and the insert/returning usage accordingly (look for the handler block that calls context.db.insert(book).values(...).returning()). - Around line 166-187: The update handler currently returns only the book columns from context.db.update(book)...returning(), but zBook() expects the related genre; modify the updateById flow in the handler so the returned payload includes the genre relation — e.g. replace the single .returning() call with a query that returns the book plus its genre (either by using a returning that selects the genre fields or performing a follow-up select/join for genre), or use your ORM's returningAll()/selectWithRelations helper to include genre; update the code around the .handler, .output(zBook()), and the context.db.update(book).set(...).where(eq(book.id, input.id)).returning() usage so the response matches zBook() including genre. ```
🪄 Autofix (Beta) Fix all unresolved CodeRabbit comments on this PR: - [ ] Push a commit to this branch (recommended) - [ ] Create a new PR with the fixes

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Potential unique constraint violation on random emails.**

Faker-generated emails may collide with existing emails in the database or with each other in the same batch. This will cause a `23505` error and abort the seed. Consider using `.onConflictDoNothing()` or generating deterministic unique emails.



<details>
<summary>🛠️ Suggested fix: add conflict handling</summary>

```diff
-      await db.insert(user).values({
+      await db.insert(user).values({
         name: faker.person.fullName(),
         email: faker.internet.email().toLowerCase(),
         emailVerified: true,
         role: 'user',
-      });
+      }).onConflictDoNothing();
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
      await db.insert(user).values({
        name: faker.person.fullName(),
        email: faker.internet.email().toLowerCase(),
        emailVerified: true,
        role: 'user',
      }).onConflictDoNothing();
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@drizzle/seed/user.ts` around lines 20 - 25, In the seed loop inserting into
the users table using db.insert(user).values(...), Faker-generated emails can
collide causing a unique constraint 23505 and abort the seed; update the insert
to handle conflicts by calling the query builder's conflict resolution (e.g.,
chain .onConflictDoNothing() or equivalent on the db.insert(user) call) or
ensure emails are deterministic/unique before inserting so duplicate email
errors are skipped without throwing.
```

</details>

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment by CodeRabbit -->

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Cursor filter logic flaw in `getUserSessions` pagination.**

The `cursorFilter` uses `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` on line 293, which includes all rows with `createdAt <= cursorCreatedAt`. This makes the tie-breaker condition `and(eq(...), gte(...))` on lines 289-292 redundant, since `lte` already matches when `createdAt` equals the cursor's timestamp.

For `DESC createdAt` ordering, the filter should use `lt` (strictly less than) for `createdAt`, so the tie-breaker applies only when timestamps are equal:



<details>
<summary>🐛 Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
       const cursorFilter = cursorRow
         ? or(
-            // desc createdAt: next page has older createdAt
-            // include cursor row, so use lte
-            // and tie-break with id ascending
             and(
               eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
               gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)
             ),
-            lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)
+            lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)
           )
         : undefined;
```

Also add `lt` to the imports on line 3.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@src/server/routers/user.ts` around lines 284 - 295, The cursorFilter in
getUserSessions incorrectly uses lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)
which causes the tie-breaker and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)) to be redundant; change the second branch to
lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt) so only strictly older createdAt rows
are matched and the gte(session.id, ...) tie-breaker is applied when timestamps
equal, and add lt to the imported operators (alongside eq, gte, lte, or, and) so
the code compiles.
```

</details>

**8 issues found** across 28 files

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name="src/server/db/schema.ts">

<violation number="1" location="src/server/db/schema.ts:58">
P2: Add an index on `session.userId` to support the new session listing filter efficiently. This route now queries sessions by `userId`, and without an index those reads will become table scans as the table grows.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="src/server/db/schema.ts:67">
P2: Add indexes for `account.userId` and `verification.identifier` to avoid auth-query performance regressions.</violation>

<violation number="3" location="src/server/db/schema.ts:105">
P2: `verification.createdAt` and `verification.updatedAt` are nullable; make them `notNull()` to match the expected auth schema and prevent null timestamp rows.</violation>
```

![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The keyset pagination logic for `getUserSessions` is incorrect for the `desc(createdAt), asc(id)` sort order. Using `lte(session.createdAt, ...)` in the `or` condition effectively swallows the tie-breaker logic because any row with an equal `createdAt` will satisfy the `lte` condition regardless of its `id`. This will result in duplicate items appearing across pages when multiple sessions share the same `createdAt` timestamp. The second part of the `or` condition should use `lt` instead of `lte` to correctly handle the tie-breaker.

```typescript
      const cursorFilter = cursorRow
        ? or(
            lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
            and(
              eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
              gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)
            )
          )
        : undefined;
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The corrected pagination logic in `getUserSessions` requires the `lt` operator, which is currently missing from the `drizzle-orm` imports.

```suggestion
import { and, asc, desc, eq, gt, gte, ilike, lt, lte, or, sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

This seeding logic performs an N+1 query pattern by calling `db.query.book.findFirst` inside a loop for every book in the dataset. This is inefficient for larger datasets. Since the `book` table has a unique constraint on `(title, author)`, it is recommended to use `.onConflictDoNothing()` on a bulk insert instead of checking for existence manually in each iteration.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Parsing database error messages using regex on the `detail` string is fragile as it depends on the PostgreSQL server's locale and version. While this works for standard English error messages, a more robust approach would be to handle constraint violations by checking the `constraint` property directly if the constraint names are known and stable, or using a specialized library for PostgreSQL error parsing.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Foreign key columns like `genreId` in the `book` table (and `userId` in `session`/`account`) should have explicit indexes for performance. Prisma automatically creates indexes for relation fields, but Drizzle requires them to be defined explicitly. Without these indexes, joins and filtered queries on these columns will result in full table scans as the database grows. Consider adding `index('book_genre_id_idx').on(table.genreId)` to the table definition.

🟠 **High** `routers/user.ts:285` The `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` condition in the `cursorFilter` is too broad. With `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`, items with the same `createdAt` but lower `id` than the cursor are incorrectly included in the next page, causing duplicates. For example, if the cursor is at `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 2)`, the `lte` matches `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 1)` which comes before the cursor. Use `lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` instead since the equal case is already handled by the `and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id))` clause.
🚀 Reply "fix it for me" or copy this AI Prompt for your agent: ```text In file src/server/routers/user.ts around line 285: The `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` condition in the `cursorFilter` is too broad. With `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`, items with the same `createdAt` but lower `id` than the cursor are incorrectly included in the next page, causing duplicates. For example, if the cursor is at `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 2)`, the `lte` matches `(createdAt: 2024-01-05, id: 1)` which comes before the cursor. Use `lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` instead since the equal case is already handled by the `and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id))` clause. Evidence trail: src/server/routers/user.ts lines 270-310 at REVIEWED_COMMIT: cursor filter uses `or(and(eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt), gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)), lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt))` with `orderBy: [desc(session.createdAt), asc(session.id)]`. The `lte` in the second OR branch matches rows with equal createdAt regardless of id, bypassing the tie-break condition in the first branch. ```

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Potential unique constraint violation on random emails.**

Faker-generated emails may collide with existing emails in the database or with each other in the same batch. This will cause a `23505` error and abort the seed. Consider using `.onConflictDoNothing()` or generating deterministic unique emails.



<details>
<summary>🛠️ Suggested fix: add conflict handling</summary>

```diff
-      await db.insert(user).values({
+      await db.insert(user).values({
         name: faker.person.fullName(),
         email: faker.internet.email().toLowerCase(),
         emailVerified: true,
         role: 'user',
-      });
+      }).onConflictDoNothing();
```
</details>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
      await db.insert(user).values({
        name: faker.person.fullName(),

        emailVerified: true,
        role: 'user',
      }).onConflictDoNothing();
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Cursor filter logic flaw in `getUserSessions` pagination.**

The `cursorFilter` uses `lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)` on line 293, which includes all rows with `createdAt <= cursorCreatedAt`. This makes the tie-breaker condition `and(eq(...), gte(...))` on lines 289-292 redundant, since `lte` already matches when `createdAt` equals the cursor's timestamp.

For `DESC createdAt` ordering, the filter should use `lt` (strictly less than) for `createdAt`, so the tie-breaker applies only when timestamps are equal:



<details>
<summary>🐛 Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
       const cursorFilter = cursorRow
         ? or(
-            // desc createdAt: next page has older createdAt
-            // include cursor row, so use lte
-            // and tie-break with id ascending
             and(
               eq(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt),
               gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)
             ),
-            lte(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)
+            lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt)
           )
         : undefined;
```

Also add `lt` to the imports on line 3.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
gte(session.id, cursorRow.id)) to be redundant; change the second branch to
lt(session.createdAt, cursorRow.createdAt) so only strictly older createdAt rows
are matched and the gte(session.id, ...) tie-breaker is applied when timestamps
```
